title: The Wolf and the Lamb

characters.name: The Wolf
characters.role: The powerful predator who invents pretexts to justify what he intends to do anyway

characters.name: The Lamb
characters.role: The innocent who answers each accusation truthfully but cannot escape the wolf's purpose

summary: A wolf finds a lamb drinking at a stream and accuses it of muddying the water. The lamb points out that he is downstream and could not be muddying the water. The wolf invents another accusation, then another, until he finally drops the pretense entirely and devours the lamb.
full_text:
A wolf came to a stream to drink, and saw a lamb at the same stream a little further down. The wolf was hungry, but he wished to find some pretense to justify what he intended to do.

'How dare you,' said the wolf in a loud voice, 'muddy the water that I am drinking?'

The lamb looked up in fear. 'But sir,' he answered politely, 'I cannot be muddying your water. I am downstream from you. The water flows from you to me, not from me to you.'

The wolf was angry to be caught in a foolish accusation. He thought for a moment. 'Perhaps that is true,' he said. 'But last year you insulted me. I have not forgotten it.'

'Last year I had not been born, sir,' said the lamb.

The wolf grew angrier. 'Then it must have been your father.'

'I do not know my father, sir,' said the lamb. 'I only know my mother.'

The wolf seemed to think a moment longer. Then he said: 'It does not matter. The truth is, I want to eat you. And I will eat you. The reasons I have given are unimportant. I should not have bothered with them at all.'

And he sprang on the lamb and devoured him.

The lesson, said the old people who told this fable, is that the powerful do not need true reasons to harm the weak. They invent reasons because the appearance of justice pleases them. But strip away the reasons, and what remains is the appetite. The lamb's only defense was the truth, and the truth is no defense at all against an appetite that has decided what it wants.
themes: the futility of reason against appetite
moral: The tyrant always finds a pretext for his tyranny. Reason is no defense against an appetite that has decided.

source.citation: Aesop, traditional. Perry Index #155. Public domain. Standard English: Townsend, Aesop's Fables (1867); Vernon Jones, Aesop's Fables (1912).

cultural_notes: This is one of the most politically pointed of Aesop's fables and has been read for two thousand years as a commentary on tyranny — the way the powerful invent justifications for what they intended to do anyway. La Fontaine's French version (Le Loup et l'Agneau, 1668) is one of his most famous fables. Phaedrus (1st century CE Latin) explicitly framed the fable as a commentary on those 'who oppress the innocent under invented pretexts.' The wolf in this fable is not a mythological pathfinder — he is a stand-in for the tyrant, and the fable is one of the foundational European meditations on the asymmetry of power.
